intro.title: Lists
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Lists are ordered, mutable sequences: `nums = [1, 2, 3]`. Index from 0; negative indices from end.

Methods: append, extend, insert, pop, remove, sort. Lists can hold mixed types.

Lists are workhorses for collections you change often.

intro.title: Tuples
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Tuples are ordered, immutable sequences: `(1, 2)`. Use for fixed records and dict keys.

Packing/unpacking: `a, b = 1, 2`. Parentheses optional for simple tuples.

Immutability protects data you should not change accidentally.
intro.sandboxCode:
point = (3, 4)
x, y = point
print(x + y)

challenges.title: Expert: immutable
challenges.promptMarkdown: cannot assign t[0]=0 — verify type tuple

questions.prompt: Tuple as dict key works because:

intro.title: Dictionaries
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Dicts map keys to values: `{"name": "Ada", "age": 36}`. Keys must be hashable; lookup is fast.

Methods: keys, values, items, get, pop, update. Dicts preserve insertion order (3.7+).

Use dicts for records, counts, caches, and JSON-like data.

intro.title: Sets
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Sets are unordered collections of unique hashable items: `{1, 2, 3}`.

Use for membership tests, removing duplicates, and set math: union, intersection, difference.

Sets are mutable; frozenset is immutable.

questions.prompt: Expert: discard vs remove on missing:

intro.title: List Comprehensions
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`[expr for item in iterable if cond]` builds lists concisely.

Often clearer and faster than manual append loops. Can nest for matrix patterns.

Readable comprehensions beat clever one-liners that harm clarity.
intro.sandboxCode:
squares = [n * n for n in range(6) if n % 2 == 0]
print(squares)

validation.setupCode: l=[x for x in range(5) if x%2==0]
l=[x for x in range(5) if x>2]
n=len([(i,j) for i in range(2) for j in range(2)])
f=[x for row in [[1],[2]] for x in row]
l=["Fizz" if i%3==0 else i for i in [3]]

intro.title: Dict and Set Comprehensions
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`{k: v for ...}` builds dicts; `{expr for ...}` builds sets.

Same syntax rules as list comprehensions with different braces.

Great for transforming data pipelines in one expression.
intro.sandboxCode:
idx = {name: len(name) for name in ["Ada", "Bob"]}
unique = {c.lower() for c in "AaBb"}

intro.title: Slicing and Indexing
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Index single elements; slices `start:stop:step` extract subsequences. Slices return new lists for lists; strings stay strings.

Omit start/stop for beginnings/ends. `::-1` reverses.

Out-of-range index raises IndexError; slices clamp gracefully.

intro.title: Iterating Collections
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Iterate dict keys (default), values, items. Use enumerate for index+value, zip for parallel iteration.

for loops work on any iterable. Avoid modifying a list while iterating over it directly.

Choosing the right iteration pattern prevents bugs and improves readability.

challenges.title: Hard: enumerate
challenges.starterCode:
for i,x in enumerate(["a","b"]):
 if x=="b": break

validation.setupCode:
s=0
for a,b in zip([1,2],[3,4]): s+=a+b

challenges.title: Expert: reversed

intro.title: Nested Data Structures
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Real data mixes lists of dicts, dicts of lists, JSON trees, and matrices as lists of lists.

Access with chained indexing: `data[0]["name"]`. Copy carefully — aliasing nested mutables surprises beginners.

Design structure to match your problem: rows as dicts vs tuples vs lists.

challenges.title: Hard: sum matrix
challenges.starterCode:
for row in [[1,2],[3,4]]:
 for v in row: s+=v

challenges.title: Hard: find in list dict
challenges.starterCode:
for u in [{"name":"Ada"}]:
 if u["name"]=="Ada": found=u["name"]

intro.title: Choosing the Right Structure
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Pick list for ordered mutable sequences; tuple for fixed records; dict for key lookup; set for uniqueness and set ops.

Consider ordering, mutability, hashability, memory, and what operations dominate.

Refactoring to the right structure early saves pain in larger programs.
intro.sandboxCode:
# list: order + change
# tuple: fixed point
# dict: lookup by key
# set: unique membership
print(type([]), type(()), type({}), type(set()))
